title: Development Arrested

jokes.text: Today, it looks like I succeeded. - So why is this man crying?
jokes.setup: Michael being honored by his family for keeping them together
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's crying despite his apparent success
jokes.explanation: Classic Arrested Development irony - success is actually a trap. Efficient structure builds curiosity then delivers the dark punchline.

jokes.text: Because now he'll have to stay with these people for a long, long time.
jokes.setup: Why is the successful man crying?
jokes.punchline: His success means being trapped with his horrible family
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - the central premise that family success is actually imprisonment. Perfect character integration for Michael's arc.

jokes.text: It was the monster called lust, to which he'd recently succumbed.
jokes.setup: George Michael being afraid of a monster
jokes.punchline: The 'monster' is lust, playing on innocent vs. adult interpretation
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection using childish language for adult content, but not exceptional.

jokes.text: And although he'd only gotten to second base... he'd gone in head first, like Pete Rose.
jokes.setup: Sexual baseball metaphor
jokes.punchline: Pete Rose reference works both sexually and literally (head-first sliding)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant layered wordplay - Pete Rose was known for aggressive sliding AND gambling, works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: And there you are, Maeby, coming out of your mother's third base.
jokes.setup: Explaining genetics using baseball metaphor
jokes.punchline: Accidentally making graphic sexual reference about birth
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tobias character moment - his oblivious sexual double entendres. High craft for character integration.

jokes.text: What, I mean, so we can make out every night? Does that not work for your schedule?
jokes.setup: George Michael suggesting they can't sleep in same room
jokes.punchline: Maeby casually scheduling their inappropriate relationship
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven humor - Maeby's casual attitude toward taboo situation creates discomfort comedy.

jokes.text: Actually, I've been wrestling with some pretty taboo...
jokes.setup: Michael asking about girls in George Michael's life
jokes.punchline: George Michael about to confess incestuous feelings, gets cut off
jokes.explanation: Good tension building - audience knows what he's about to say, dramatic irony creates discomfort.

jokes.text: Risky! There it is, we're doing it, huh?
jokes.setup: Waiting to hear stock recommendation
jokes.punchline: Michael excited about mediocre 'risky' rating
jokes.explanation: Character-based humor about Michael's low expectations, but fairly standard execution.

jokes.text: Family. - I was gonna say breakfast, but...
jokes.setup: Michael asking what's most important
jokes.punchline: Michael's answer isn't as noble as expected
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection joke undermining Michael's attempt at wisdom. Solid but not special.

jokes.text: I've heard that a couple times too.
jokes.setup: Michael promising they'll talk things out later
jokes.punchline: George Michael noting this is an empty promise Michael makes repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Good character observation - shows George Michael is aware of his father's avoidance patterns.

jokes.text: Look at that, Buster. You would have lost a hand anyway.
jokes.setup: Buster's missing hand being referenced
jokes.punchline: Lucille's cruel observation that he was doomed either way
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille cruelty - dismissing Buster's trauma with dark logic. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I've always pictured him in a lighthouse.
jokes.setup: Family discussing where Gob lives
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's random, oddly specific mental image
jokes.explanation: Absurd specificity that feels very Lindsay - random but somehow fitting for Gob's dramatic nature.

jokes.text: We can do that?
jokes.setup: Michael mentioning they could sell shares and cash out
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's immediate mercenary interest
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Lindsay's priorities, but predictable execution.

jokes.text: It's the only hair he's got. What? - He's an alpaca. - He has alopecia.
jokes.setup: Discussing Stan Sitwell's appearance
jokes.punchline: Confusion between alpaca and alopecia, with cruel piling on
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay confusion with characteristic family cruelty toward outsiders.

jokes.text: Two million! Two million! That sounds like a lot more than it actually is.
jokes.setup: Lindsay excited about two million dollars
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately deflating her enthusiasm with reality
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Lindsay's materialism and Michael's pragmatism, though execution is straightforward.

jokes.text: You've already spent $1,200 on eggs, and that would be it forever.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining why two million isn't much
jokes.punchline: Specific, absurd example of Lindsay's spending habits
jokes.explanation: Specific absurd detail that perfectly captures Lindsay's ridiculous spending. Good character specificity.

jokes.text: We can't have kids. What's the matter... What are you say... It's not even an option really.
jokes.setup: Michael talking about building something for George Michael and Maeby's kids
jokes.punchline: Their panicked reaction revealing their romantic relationship
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy from their panic, creates dramatic irony since audience knows their secret.

jokes.text: Might wanna start worrying about the table.
jokes.setup: Tobias saying he won't worry about food on the table
jokes.punchline: Gob pointing out they don't even have a table (presumably eating on floor/bed)
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag inference about their poverty, economical delivery.

jokes.text: You know, being twins, our birthdays are pretty close to one another.
jokes.setup: Lindsay complaining about turning 40
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's complete misunderstanding of what twins means
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly stupid Lindsay moment - misunderstanding twins have the SAME birthday. Perfect character integration and absurd logic.

jokes.text: as she does of getting attacked by a shark. Oh, I'm sorry. I thought you were going to say seal.
jokes.setup: Gob making cruel comparison about Lindsay's marriage prospects
jokes.punchline: Lindsay expecting seal reference due to Buster's trauma
jokes.explanation: Good callback integration showing Lindsay expects family cruelty to reference Buster's specific trauma.

jokes.text: Girls just grow faster than boys. Dinner's ready. We're having Lindsay chops.
jokes.setup: George Sr. defending calling Lindsay fat as a child
jokes.punchline: Lucille continuing the cruelty with cannibal joke
jokes.explanation: Dark family cruelty perfectly in character - parents casually traumatizing their daughter with food/body issues.

jokes.text: What? I want her to be prepared in case some bully at school is as clever as I am. No bully ever would be.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explaining his cruel nickname for Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Lucille's backhanded compliment/insult to her husband
jokes.explanation: Good marital cruelty - Lucille simultaneously defending and insulting George Sr. while dismissing his cleverness.

jokes.text: It wasn't. Although they marketed the idea better... the Frozen Banana itself was the brainchild of a Korean immigrant who threatened to sue them.
jokes.setup: Revelation that the banana stand wasn't their idea
jokes.punchline: They stole it from an immigrant who they then had deported
jokes.explanation: Brilliant recontextualization of iconic banana stand - reveals the family's casual racism and theft as origin story.

jokes.text: Where his business never recovered.
jokes.setup: Korean immigrant was deported
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that deportation ruined his business
jokes.explanation: Darkly understated observation about the consequences of the family's cruelty.

jokes.text: I'm concerned about you. Because I do not want to see... - He'll be fine. - You'll be fine.
jokes.setup: George Sr. expressing concern about Lucille's control over Michael
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately demonstrating that control by finishing his sentence and making Michael agree
jokes.explanation: Perfect demonstration of the very behavior being warned about - Lucille's control is so complete she can manipulate the conversation in real time.

jokes.text: Lighthouse wasn't far off.
jokes.setup: Lindsay's earlier comment about picturing Gob in a lighthouse
jokes.punchline: Reveals Gob actually lives on a boat, which is lighthouse-adjacent
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff - Lindsay's random guess was surprisingly accurate in spirit.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, somebody's drawing a little one on those checks. No big deal.
jokes.setup: Gob claiming Michael gives him 1,500 when Michael says 500
jokes.punchline: Gob admits to altering the checks by adding a '1' in front
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven humor - Gob casually admitting to check fraud like it's no big deal.

jokes.text: You give a man a fish, he'll eat for a night. - If you teach a man to fish... - He'll wanna use your yacht.
jokes.setup: Classic 'teach a man to fish' proverb setup
jokes.punchline: Gob's selfish twist on the wisdom
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of familiar wisdom - perfectly captures Gob's self-centered worldview.

jokes.text: How would it feel to lose the company to a man who has less hair on his !@%$ than I have on my !@%$#.
jokes.setup: Lucille arguing against letting Gob sell shares
jokes.punchline: Extremely crude comparison about Stan Sitwell's baldness
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Lucille vulgarity, though relies on shock value rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: It can't be worse than hearing that just was.
jokes.setup: Lucille's crude comparison
jokes.punchline: Michael's disgusted reaction to his mother's vulgarity
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Michael's ongoing horror at his family's behavior.

jokes.text: How long has this been going on? - I don't know... 53 weeks?
jokes.setup: Michael asking duration of the inappropriate relationship
jokes.punchline: George Michael's oddly specific and lengthy timeframe
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing George Michael has been tracking this precisely, adding to the awkwardness.

jokes.text: Yeah, Ann is the girl for us.
jokes.setup: Michael suggesting George Michael reconnect with Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael's weird phrasing suggests his own interest in the teenage girl
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable phrasing that suggests Michael's inappropriate interest, creating cringe comedy.

jokes.text: I hope that's not a crack about my hair color, lips, forehead, nose and teeth.
jokes.setup: Sitwell mentioning natural children
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's paranoid list of all her cosmetic work
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Lindsay's insecurity about all her cosmetic modifications.

jokes.text: It's alpaca actually. Cruelty-free. Really narrows your choices.
jokes.setup: Lindsay's insult about his rayon sweater set face
jokes.punchline: Sitwell's practical concern about ethical hair replacement options
jokes.explanation: Clever deadpan response - Sitwell's practical concerns about ethical toupee materials is unexpectedly charming.

jokes.text: Are you kidding me? This is the happiest I've been since the day I got my new nose.
jokes.setup: Learning she's adopted
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's joy at not being a Bluth, comparing to cosmetic surgery happiness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Lindsay values appearance changes and freedom from family equally highly.

jokes.text: Uh, wait, three-year-old? You said it was 37 years ago. Oh, yeah, right. Hey, we should do something for your 40th next week.
jokes.setup: Math doesn't add up for Lindsay's adoption age
jokes.punchline: Sitwell casually revealing Lindsay is actually 40, not 37
jokes.explanation: Good reveal that catches both Lindsay and audience off-guard - the math exposes her age lies.

jokes.text: Be gentle, she's almost 40!
jokes.setup: Lindsay attacking Sitwell after age revelation
jokes.punchline: Sitwell using her age as reason for mercy
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the age joke with practical application during a fight.

jokes.text: This is the biggest thing to happen since Uniprod hired that wee-brain to run drama development.
jokes.setup: Executive excited about Maeby's story
jokes.punchline: Meta reference to hiring an actual child to run development
jokes.explanation: Self-referential humor about the show's own premise, though somewhat inside-baseball.

jokes.text: I'm not your sister. - What? Hey, Lindsay. - I'm adopted. And I'm three years older.
jokes.setup: Lindsay drunkenly approaching Michael
jokes.punchline: Lindsay trying to use adoption news to seduce her brother
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe comedy - Lindsay's desperation leads to pursuing her adopted brother romantically.

jokes.text: I'm just not that into older women.
jokes.setup: Lindsay's romantic proposition
jokes.punchline: Michael rejecting her for age rather than the obvious reason
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - Michael's rejection focuses on the least problematic aspect of this proposition. Perfect character-driven comedy.

jokes.text: No, I mean, you can't spoon me like that.
jokes.setup: Tobias saying he can't sell shares
jokes.punchline: Michael clarifying he means the spooning position, not the business
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag showing the awkwardness of their sleeping arrangement, though execution is straightforward.

jokes.text: He also mentioned that we all only had three more weeks on Earth and that fossils were just something the Jews buried in 1924.
jokes.setup: Ann's Uncle Paul giving information about Ann
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of his completely insane religious beliefs
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing the extreme religious fundamentalism in Ann's family.

jokes.text: Adopting a child we didn't want just to stick it to a competitor? Oh, that would make us look great.
jokes.setup: Michael asking why they didn't reveal Lindsay was adopted
jokes.punchline: Lucille's sarcasm reveals the cruel motivation while trying to justify secrecy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille logic - using sarcasm to inadvertently admit to terrible motivations.

jokes.text: We loved her just as much as any of you normal kids. More than Gob.
jokes.setup: Lucille claiming they loved Lindsay equally
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that they loved her more than their actual son Gob
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Lucille's casual cruelty toward Gob while trying to be reassuring.

jokes.text: So, it's embezzlement, bribery and conspiracy? And a whole lot of love. All right, and perjury.
jokes.setup: Michael listing the crimes in Lucille's plan
jokes.punchline: Lucille adding 'love' to the list, Michael adding yet another crime
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of criminal activity with Lucille's twisted addition and Michael's resignation.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no, no. It's a chain of popular nightclubs. Although I haven't been to this particular location. - It's on the Long Beach Harbor. - That's the ship.
jokes.setup: Tobias booking venue called Queen Mary
jokes.punchline: Tobias accidentally booking the actual ship despite fear requirement
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven error showing Tobias's incompetence accidentally creating the exact problem they wanted to avoid.

jokes.text: Hot Sailors. Better yet, Hot Se... - I like Hot Sailors. - Mm, me too.
jokes.setup: Tobias thinking of nautical themed strippers
jokes.punchline: Cutting himself off before saying something worse, family approving anyway
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Tobias accidental innuendo with family's oblivious approval.

jokes.text: What? I thought that's what all you guys lived for. While Gob debased everything they believed in.
jokes.setup: Ann's friends trying to teach Gob about Holy Trinity
jokes.punchline: Gob's complete misunderstanding and subsequent corruption of their faith
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Gob's selfish interpretation of religious concepts.

jokes.text: Yes, she is my sister, no matter what any piece of paper says.
jokes.setup: Michael giving speech about family unity
jokes.punchline: Inadvertently acknowledging the adoption while trying to dismiss it
jokes.explanation: Good irony - Michael trying to be inclusive while inadvertently highlighting the very thing that makes Lindsay feel excluded.

jokes.text: It wasn't exactly a turn-on.
jokes.setup: Michael crying during his emotional speech
jokes.punchline: Narrator's understated observation about the unsexy nature of emotional breakdown
jokes.explanation: Understated humor deflating the emotional moment, though not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Never seen a CEO cry like that before, except at his sentencing.
jokes.setup: Michael's emotional breakdown at the podium
jokes.punchline: Sitwell's comparison to criminal CEOs at sentencing
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor connecting Michael's breakdown to the show's corporate crime themes.

jokes.text: I've made a huge mistake.
jokes.setup: Michael realizing how much he's compromised
jokes.punchline: Gob's signature catchphrase delivered by Michael
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback - Michael using Gob's signature line at his moment of realization elevates both characters and provides perfect series bookend.

jokes.text: Ah, this is why they dropped those other charges. You gave me up, George. - George? I'm not George.
jokes.setup: George Sr. being arrested and assuming Oscar betrayed him
jokes.punchline: Oscar's confused response reveals mistaken identity
jokes.explanation: Good character confusion moment showing how the twins get mistaken even by law enforcement.

jokes.text: Oscar, when are you going to learn there's no such thing as free shrimp?
jokes.setup: Oscar being at the party despite not being invited
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s exasperated wisdom about Oscar's freeloading
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific wisdom - perfectly captures the dynamic between the twins with quotable line.

jokes.text: Annyong? - Annyong. But my real name is... - Hello. - Hello? - Annyong.
jokes.setup: Revelation that Annyong was working against the family
jokes.punchline: The confusing name reveal that everyone's been calling him by his greeting
jokes.explanation: Excellent long-term payoff revealing the true meaning behind Annyong's constant greeting and his secret mission.

jokes.text: Because my grandfather vowed one day he would get even for banana stand you stole from him.
jokes.setup: Long-running mystery of Annyong's presence in the family
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been plotting revenge for the banana stand theft all along
jokes.explanation: Masterful long-term payoff connecting multiple storylines. Reveals Annyong's true purpose in elaborate callback structure.

jokes.text: I don't even wanna tell you what these guys thought that meant.
jokes.setup: Lucille asking if the men know how to shovel coal
jokes.punchline: Implication that they misunderstood this as sexual proposition
jokes.explanation: Low-level sexual humor that relies on implication rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: This is degrading. I thought we were gonna strip.
jokes.setup: Hot Cops having to actually help with ship escape
jokes.punchline: Stripper complaining that real work is more degrading than stripping
jokes.explanation: Good ironic humor about professional pride - stripper finds manual labor beneath his dignity.

jokes.text: Oh, come on!
jokes.setup: Buster facing his second biggest fear (water)
jokes.punchline: Then immediately encountering his first biggest fear (seal)
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and callback - Buster's perfect comic timing and the cruel irony of facing both fears consecutively.

jokes.text: No, I, uh... I don't see it as a series. Maybe a movie.
jokes.setup: Maeby pitching her family's story as TV show
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard (the show's narrator/producer) suggesting it should be a movie instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor - Ron Howard essentially rejecting his own TV show in favor of the movie that would eventually get made.

tmdb_overview: After returning home from Iraq, George Sr. is finally cleared of all charges, and Michael is relieved to be the head of a successful company for a change. The real brains behind the madness is revealed, and just as the family begins to celebrate their victory, a whole new set of problems emerge.
insight: Season 3 finale delivers 60 jokes in 60 minutes—the show's most balanced density before the creative collapse.
